1:
The building of the temple opens on a date the witnesses treat as one of the fixed points of Old Testament chronology, and their first labour is to defend it. Keil and Delitzsch set the frame, reading the verse as the heading of a whole account — the date, then the plan and size of the house, then the divine promise, then the internal fittings. The reformed Gill and the puritan Matthew Henry accept the four hundred and eightieth year without hesitation and reckon it the same way: forty years in the wilderness, seventeen under Joshua, some two hundred and ninety-nine under the judges, forty under Eli, forty under Samuel and Saul, forty under David, and four of Solomon's reign — arriving, as Henry says, at 'just the sum of 480.'

Against this stands a crowd of rival reckonings, and the grammatical-historical commentators meet them head on. Adam Clarke parades more than a dozen conflicting figures — Josephus's 592, the Septuagint's 440, Glycas's 330, and others ranging up to 680 — and concludes drily that the number 'in the common Hebrew text is as likely to be the true one as any of the others.' Charles Ellicott mounts the fullest defence: the date is given 'with marked precision' and forms an epoch answering to the equally formal dating of the Exodus in Exodus 12:40–41. He weighs the Septuagint's 440 as a possible scribal interchange of similar Hebrew letters, and Josephus's 592 as perhaps a tacit harmonizing with some heathen reckoning; but the vague, rounded notices of the Judges — possibly overlapping judgeships — cannot, he argues, overturn so deliberate a statement, and neither Paul's rounded '450 years' in Acts 13:19–21 nor the compressed genealogies of Ruth 4:18–22 settles the matter against it.

The debate over the exact span does not touch the verse's other note, on which the witnesses agree. Clarke observes that the month Zif answers to part of April and May, and that the very names of the months here — Zif, and Bul later — were borrowed from the Chaldeans, evidence that the book took its final form after the Babylonian captivity, since before Solomon the Israelites numbered their months rather than naming them.

Henry alone draws the pastoral moral out of the long delay. That the holy house was built only 480 years after the holy nation was founded he reads as deliberate: Israel had by their sins made themselves unworthy of the honour, and God 'was in no haste for a temple,' showing how little he values external pomp in his service. David's plain tent had been called the house of the Lord (2 Samuel 12:20) and 'served as well as Solomon's temple'; the new house mattered chiefly because it was to be a shadow of good things to come (Hebrews 9:9). The precision of the date thus honours a mercy long deferred.
2:
With the dimensions of the house the witnesses turn from chronology to architecture, and the most illuminating observation comes from the grammatical-historical side. Charles Ellicott lays down the governing principle of the whole design: by comparison with Exodus 26:16–23, the temple proved 'an exact copy of the Tabernacle, each dimension being doubled,' and therefore eight times its cubical bulk. Yet doubling a small original still yields a small building — 'whatever measure we take for the cubit,' about ninety feet long, thirty wide, and forty-five high, 'not larger than a good-sized parish church.' From this he draws the point that governs everything that follows: the temple 'relied for magnificence not on size, but on costliness of material and wealth of decoration.' The house was a shrine for the ministering priests, not an auditorium for the congregation, who gathered in the courts.

Ellicott presses the resemblance to the tabernacle further than mere proportion. He finds the plan carried over into a porch (raised in 2 Chronicles 3:4 into a lofty tower), a division of the house into two rooms 'like a nave and chancel,' something like aisles opening outward, clerestory windows, and a high-pitched roof — a likeness, he suggests, deliberately preserved by the old Freemasonry that took Solomon's temple as its sacred pattern.

Keil and Delitzsch supply the metrological care the subject demands. The measures are given 'in the clear,' as the spaces were actually seen; and, following 2 Chronicles 3:3, they are reckoned 'after the earlier measure' — the old Mosaic or sacred cubit, a hand-breadth longer, by Ezekiel 40:5 and Ezekiel 43:13, than the civil cubit of the captivity. On Thenius's investigations they fix the sacred cubit near twenty and a half inches.

Adam Clarke, taking Bishop Cumberland's cubit of nearly twenty-two inches, works the sums differently, making the length some thirty-six yards, the breadth twelve, the height eighteen; but he grows cautious. Because the accounts in Kings, Chronicles, Ezekiel, and Josephus differ, and because learned reconstructions from Villalpandus to Lightfoot and Prideaux 'have produced very different buildings,' he declines to 'hazard anything' on the temple's precise appearance. Gill, briefest of all, simply notes that the house was built 'for the Lord' — for his worship, honour, and glory. The witnesses do not quarrel over the plain numbers; where they diverge is only on the length of the cubit and the wisdom of reconstructing the whole, and they agree that the building's glory lay not in its scale but in its consecration and its cost.
3:
The porch, or entrance-hall, draws the witnesses into a small but characteristic problem — a flat contradiction between the two records of its height — and they handle it with the sobriety typical of the grammatical-historical school. Keil and Delitzsch describe the hall as standing 'in the face of' the Holy Place, twenty cubits broad (matching the width of the house) and ten cubits deep before it. The height our text omits; but 2 Chronicles 3:4 gives it as a hundred and twenty cubits, and this Keil judges 'certainly an error.' His reasoning is architectural: a structure of such a height 'could not possibly have been designated' a porch or hall, but would have been called a tower. Against Ewald and Stieglitz, who defend the figure, he holds that the disproportion is decisive.

Charles Ellicott reaches the same verdict by a different road. He gives the porch as thirty feet wide and fifteen deep, and notes that the hundred-and-twenty-cubit height — followed by some manuscripts of the Septuagint and by Josephus — comes to a hundred and eighty feet, a measure 'hardly in accordance with anything else known of ancient architecture.' Yet, ever alert to the medieval parallel that runs through his reading of the temple, he adds that such a soaring frontage is 'not at all unlike the western tower of a Gothic church' — allowing that a raised entrance-tower over a low, broad porch is at least conceivable, even if the precise number be corrupt.

Gill contributes only the orientation, the detail the others assume: the porch 'stood at the east end' of the house, before the temple proper. That single note quietly fixes the worshipper's approach — from the east, through the porch, toward the Holy Place and, beyond it, the innermost shrine — the same eastward-facing plan the tabernacle had kept before it. On the substance the three are wholly agreed: the porch was as wide as the house and shallow in depth, a vestibule rather than a room, and the towering height assigned it in Chronicles is to be treated as a copyist's slip rather than a rival tradition. Their instinct is conservative in both directions — they will not lightly emend a received number, but neither will they defend a figure that the nature of the building itself forbids — and here the building forbids it. The porch was a threshold, and a threshold it remains, its modest depth of ten cubits marking the step from the outer court into the presence of God, its breadth binding it to the house whose entrance it adorned. Where the fittings that follow will grow ever more lavish, this first member of the structure is described with a plainness that lets its proportion, not its ornament, speak.
4:
The single verse on the windows opens the chapter's liveliest small dispute, and it turns, as so often, on a rare Hebrew phrase. The traditional rendering, preserved in the margin — windows 'broad within and narrow without,' splayed like the openings of a Gothic wall — is defended by Adam Clarke from the Arabic and the Targum, which he reports as windows 'open within and shut without,' wide on the inside and narrow on the outside 'such as we still see in ancient castles.' Clarke plays with the very word, deriving 'window' from 'wind-door,' a door to admit the wind and ventilate the building, and noting frankly that 'we hear nothing of glass or any other diaphanous substance.'

The grammatical-historical commentators press the other reading. Keil and Delitzsch reject the Chaldee gloss of 'broad within, narrow without' as a rendering of the words themselves: the phrase means windows 'of closed beams' — latticework that could not be opened and shut at pleasure, unlike the ordinary house-windows of 2 Kings 13:17 or Daniel 6:11 — since the same root denotes overlaid beams in 1 Kings 7:4–5. Yet Keil is fair to the tradition: given the great thickness of the walls, the openings were 'probably narrower without than within, as in the older Egyptian buildings,' so that the Chaldee interpretation, though not the true sense of the words, is 'in accordance with the fact.' Ellicott lands in the same place, calling the splayed reading well supported but judging 'windows with fixed beams' — immovable lattices like jalousies, 'useful for ventilation, but immovable, so that no one could look out or in' — the more probable meaning. Gill holds the middle: windows that 'could be both,' having shutters to open or close at pleasure.

Beneath the philology, one voice hears the verse preach. Matthew Henry, taking up the marginal 'broad within and narrow without,' turns the architecture into a mirror of the sanctified mind: such should be the eyes of our understanding, 'reflecting nearer on ourselves than on other people, looking much within, to judge ourselves, but little without, to censure our brethren.' And he reads the very narrowness of the temple's light as a parable of its age — 'the darkness of that dispensation, in comparison with the gospel day,' when the fuller light would come. So the debate over whether the lights were splayed or latticed does not divide the witnesses at heart: all grant that they let in light and air through thick walls, and Henry alone shows how even a builder's detail can be made to search the conscience and to measure one covenant against another.
5:
The side-chambers built against the temple bring out the grammatical-historical concern for exact construction and the reformed instinct to keep the plain sense clear. Keil and Delitzsch give the fullest account: Solomon built 'an outwork round about' against the two longer walls and the rear of the house — 'not against the front also, where the porch was' — so that the lower building embraced the Holy Place and the Holy of Holies but stopped short at the eastern face. They dwell on the grammar with characteristic patience, noting how the Hebrew uses the masculine collectively for the whole three-storeyed mass of side-buildings and the feminine for a single storey, a distinction 'very common in Arabic.'

Gill reaches the same picture by a plainer route. He corrects the sense of the preposition — the chambers were built not into the wall but 'near it,' or rather 'upon' it, their beams deliberately not fastened into the temple's own masonry — and he draws the necessary inference from the phrase 'round about': it 'must be understood of the two sides, north and south, and of the west end only, for at the east end, where the porch was, there were none.' The two witnesses thus arrive independently at one plan: a low, three-storeyed range of rooms wrapped around three sides of the sanctuary, structurally distinct from the holy walls they leaned against.

The care with which both note that the side-building surrounded only three sides is not idle antiquarian precision; it fixes the shape of the whole sanctuary in the reader's eye and prepares for the verse that follows, where the reason for keeping the beams clear of the wall — the rebated ledges that carried them — will be given. Here the witnesses are content to establish the arrangement. There is no dispute between them: Keil supplies the philological scaffolding, Gill the orientation and the corrected preposition, and both agree that the chambers, though built against the house 'round about,' left the porch-end open and never pierced the temple wall itself. The reticence of the text about the use of these rooms they leave for later; what matters at this point is that the magnificence of the shrine was ringed with a practical provision, the sacred core untouched and self-standing while a serviceable structure of stores and stairs grew up against it. The building's holiness and its usefulness are kept, quite literally, in separate walls — the outwork clinging to the house yet never fastened into its stones, a small architectural fact that both the reformed and the grammatical-historical readers are careful to preserve exactly as the text gives it.
6:
This verse explains the arrangement the last one described: why the encircling chambers widened as they rose, and why their beams were kept out of the temple wall. The witnesses read it with one mind, the grammatical-historical Keil supplying the mechanics and the reformed Gill the orientation.

Keil and Delitzsch lay out the graded breadths — the lowest storey five cubits, the middle six, the third seven — and account for the increase by the ledges cut into the outside of the temple wall: 'rebates were attached against the temple wall, at the point where the lower beams of the different side stories were to be placed, so that the heads of these beams rested upon the rebates and were not inserted in the actual wall.' As each storey rose, the wall stepped back, so that the ledge carrying the next tier of beams was narrower and the room correspondingly wider. Keil names these steppings 'deductions or contractions of the thickness of the wall,' and reckons there were probably four of them — three for the three floors and one for the roof.

Gill keeps to the plain statement, identifying 'the nethermost chamber' as 'the nethermost row of them, which were upon the first floor,' and so anchoring the reader in the lowest of the three tiers before the ascent begins. Between the two there is nothing to debate; they simply divide the labour, Gill fixing which storey is meant and Keil explaining the engineering that made the whole possible.

What the witnesses together preserve is the reverent logic already sounded and soon to be sounded again: the sanctuary's own walls were not to be cut, bored, or burdened. The beams of the surrounding chambers rested on external ledges rather than in sockets driven through the holy masonry, so that the temple wall stood entire and inviolate, bearing the weight of the side-building without being breached by it. It is the same principle that forbids the iron tool in the verse that follows — a determination that nothing rough or intrusive should touch the house of God — here worked out in the quiet ingenuity of a stepped wall. Matthew Henry, commenting on this stretch of the chapter, drew the church-lesson from exactly this detail: that the beams were not fastened in the walls, lest the walls be weakened, teaches us not to impair the church's strength 'under pretence of adding to its beauty or convenience.' The graded chambers, widening as they climb, are thus both a feat of building and a figure of care: usefulness added to the sanctuary without the least injury to the holiness it enclosed.
7:
Here the witnesses set down their tools and preach, for the silence of the building is the verse's whole burden — no hammer, axe, or iron tool heard while the house rose. All four commentators dwell on the reverence of it, and two press it into type. Keil and Delitzsch establish the fact against a fanciful reading: the stones were not miraculously unhewn, as Theodoret supposed, but 'so perfectly tooled and prepared when first broken in the quarry' that at the building site no iron instrument was required. The labour of shaping was simply done elsewhere.

Adam Clarke presses the practical point and then lifts it. Every stone was hewn, squared, and its very place assigned before it reached Jerusalem, so that nothing but mallets and pins were needed and 'there was no noise.' But he asks why the Spirit should mark so plain a fact, and answers with a wesleyan application that has become famous: the temple was a type of the kingdom of God, and 'the souls of men are to be prepared here for that place of blessedness.' There, he says, 'there is no preaching, exhortations, repentance, tears, cries, nor prayers; the stones must be all squared and fitted here for their place in the New Jerusalem, and, being living stones, must be built up a holy temple for a habitation of God through the Spirit.'

The grammatical-historical Ellicott grounds the same reverence in the Law. The provision, 'involving much labour, and requiring no little skill, was one of reverence,' and may have been suggested by the ban on iron tools upon the altar in Exodus 20:25 and Deuteronomy 27:5 — though he carefully notes that the altar-law forbade dressing the stones at all, lest man's art 'pollute' them, whereas here the stones were fully dressed, only in silence. He seals it with Heber's lines: 'No workman's steel, no ponderous axes rung; / Like some tall palm the noiseless fabric sprung.'

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the threads into worship. This was 'the temple of the God of peace, and therefore no iron tool must be heard in it'; 'quietness and silence both become and befriend religious exercises,' and 'God's work should be done with as much care and as little noise as may be.' He sharpens the contrast: the temple was thrown down with axes and hammers by men who 'roared in the midst of the congregation' (Psalms 74:4–6), 'but it was built up in silence,' for 'clamour and violence often hinder the work of God, but never further it.' The witnesses do not divide; from quarry-craft to conscience they agree that a house for God is built quietly, its every stone prepared beforehand, its rising a parable of the soul made ready for glory.
8:
The verse on the door of the side-chambers and their winding stair is among the plainest in the chapter, and the witnesses treat it accordingly, the grammatical-historical Keil carrying almost the whole weight and the reformed Gill adding a single point of the compass. Gill identifies 'the right side of the house' as 'the south side of it,' so that the entrance to the tiered chambers lay on the southern flank, and the reader knows at once where to look for the door that gave access to a structure otherwise sealed from the sanctuary.

Keil and Delitzsch take the winding stair — the spiral by which one climbed from storey to storey, out of the lowest into the middle and out of the middle into the third — and, characteristically, read the verse forward into the two that follow, treating the door and stair as the last exterior detail before the roofing is described. On the roofing itself their comment is precise and corrective. When the text says Solomon 'covered the house with beams and boards of cedar,' this is not the interior panelling, which is described later, but the roof; the verb 'means to conceal and cover in all the other passages,' and the word rendered 'boards' means rather 'beams,' not 'an arched covering,' since beams cut in an arch would have been something other than the straight timbers the account intends.

There is no debate here, and little for the witnesses to divide over; the interest of the verse is entirely constructional. Yet the small notice repays attention, for it completes the picture of the encircling side-building begun three verses earlier: a three-storeyed range, entered by a single door on the south, climbed by an internal spiral rather than an external ladder, and roofed — like the sanctuary it embraced — in cedar. The absence of any devotional flourish is itself telling. Where the reverent silence of the stones drew every commentator to the pulpit a verse before, the mechanics of a staircase draw only the surveyor's eye, and the witnesses are content to be surveyors. Keil's determination to render 'beams' rather than 'boards,' and to insist that the covering here is the roof and not the wainscot reserved for the interior, guards the reader from confusing the outside of the house with its inside — a confusion the very next stretch of the chapter, with its lavish cedar lining, might otherwise invite. So the verse serves as a hinge: it finishes the exterior, fixes the humble means of access to the chambers, and points ahead to the roofing that closes the shell before Solomon turns to adorn the rooms within.
9:
With the shell of the house closed, the witnesses pause over the roofing, and the most memorable comment is a small lesson in ancient building from the wesleyan Adam Clarke. Where the modern reader might picture a plastered ceiling, Clarke corrects the anachronism: 'the Eastern custom is very different from ours; we ceil with plaster, and make our floors of wood; they make their floors of plaster or painted tiles, and make their ceilings of wood.' To cover the house with cedar was therefore to give it a wooden ceiling overhead, not to sheathe its floor — and he adds that 'in ancient times our own buildings were somewhat similar,' pointing to the timber ceiling of Westminster Hall as a surviving witness to the older manner.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott fixes the sense of 'covered' against a common misreading. The word means that Solomon 'roofed the house with a roof of cedar beams and boarding thereon.' Some, he reports, have supposed that he covered the outside walls with cedar as well, 'so that the whole should still look like a wooden tabernacle'; but Ellicott judges this 'not necessarily implied, and in itself unlikely.' The cedar of this verse belongs to the roof, and the panelling of the walls waits for the interior account further on.

Gill, briefest of the three, marks the stage the verse records: 'so he built the house, and finished it' — 'the body of it, the walls of the holy and most holy place, with the chambers on the sides of them, and the porch at the end that led into them.' The structural work is now complete; what remains is adornment.

The witnesses are wholly agreed, and the value of their notes lies in what they forestall. Left to itself, the phrase 'covered the house with cedar' might tempt a reader to imagine either a cedar floor or a temple clad in wood without and within, and both Clarke and Ellicott quietly close those doors — the one by explaining the Eastern practice of wooden ceilings over tiled floors, the other by confining the cedar to the roof and refusing the picture of an outwardly timber-clad house. Gill, for his part, simply certifies that the building is now finished in its fabric. Between them they hand the reader over to the next movement of the chapter — the promise of God, and then the glittering interior of gold and carved cedar — with the outer house securely built, roofed in fragrant and durable cedar, and free of the misconceptions a hasty reading of a single verb might have bred. It is the grammatical-historical school at its most useful: not soaring, but keeping the sense honest.
10:
The verse gathers up the side-chambers a last time, giving the height the earlier notice had withheld, and the witnesses read it as the completion of the encircling structure. Keil and Delitzsch take 'five cubits' as the height of each storey — so that the three-tiered side-building rose, within, some fifteen cubits, and without, reckoning floors and roof, about eighteen or twenty. This, they observe, left the temple-house, thirty cubits high inside and about thirty-two without, rising 'about twelve or fourteen cubits above the side building,' with 'plenty of room for the windows in the side walls.' Thus the graded chambers of the previous verses are shown to reach only two-thirds of the way up the sanctuary wall, leaving the clerestory windows free above them to light the interior — the very windows whose narrow lattices the chapter had already described.

Keil also fixes the sense of the difficult clause that the chambers 'rested on the house with timber of cedar': the side-building 'held to the house' by means of cedar beams, fastened to the temple in the manner the account has been at pains to describe — resting on the external ledges, not driven into the holy wall. The whole encircling range is thereby both bound securely to the sanctuary and kept clear of its masonry.

Gill supplies the sequence, taking these as 'the upper chambers,' built upon the beams of cedar with which the house was covered, so that the three storeys 'raised the whole house to an equal height with the porch.' He cannot resist a devotional glance forward: in one of these chambers, he suggests, the disciples may have gathered after the ascension — a lightly-offered typological leap from the fabric of the old house to the beginnings of the new. (A stray note attached here to the passage, drawn from an unrelated account of the school of the prophets at Geba, belongs to another chapter and casts no light on Solomon's building.)

The witnesses do not contend. Keil furnishes the geometry that makes the whole elevation intelligible — the low, tiered outwork against a taller sanctuary, windows clear above it — and Gill the narrative sense and a hint of type. Together they close the description of the exterior on a note of proportion: nothing haphazard, the heights carefully graded so that the surrounding rooms should not choke the light, and the sacred core still standing head and shoulders above its serviceable girdle of chambers. The exterior finished and measured to the last cubit, the chapter is ready to break off its architecture and record the word that came to Solomon in the midst of the work.
11:
The narrative of building is interrupted by a word from God, and the witnesses fasten on both the manner of its coming and the weight of its condition. On the manner, the grammatical-historical Keil and the reformed Gill agree that a prophet is implied. Keil notes that the phrase 'the word of the Lord came' 'seems to point to a prophetic medium,' the more so since 1 Kings 9:2 tells us God appeared to Solomon in person only twice; Gill reports that some Jewish writers name Ahijah the Shilonite as the messenger, whether the word came 'by an impulse of the Spirit' or 'by the hand of a prophet.' Adam Clarke adds a careful distinction: this is not the later revelation of 1 Kings 9:2, which followed the dedication, but a word given 'while building, to encourage him, to warn him against apostasy, and to assure him of God's continued protection' if he and his house remained faithful.

On the substance the puritan Matthew Henry preaches most fully, and lands the pastoral point the others only touch. God's word here proves that 'none employ themselves for God without having his eye upon them' — 'I know thy works, thy good works.' The message serves two ends at once. By its promise it encourages a builder who might otherwise weary of the great 'care, expense, and fatigue' of the work: 'an eye to the promise will carry us cheerfully through our work.' But by its condition it sobers him, for the establishment of his house is bound to obedience — God 'plainly let him know that all this charge which he and his people were at, in erecting this temple, would neither excuse them from obedience to the law of God nor shelter them from his judgments in case of disobedience.' Henry's summary is the verse's most quoted line: 'Keeping God's commandments is better, and more pleasing to him, than building churches.'

Keil roots the promise in its Davidic source. The pledge points back to 2 Samuel 7:12 and the everlasting establishment of the throne; God would 'set up' that word for Solomon on the same terms David had urged upon him at the handing over of the kingdom in 1 Kings 2:3. The building of the house does not create a new covenant but applies the old one to this new occasion. The witnesses converge without dispute: a prophetic word, given mid-work, encouraging in its promise and searching in its condition, teaching that the costliest service earns no exemption from obedience — that God dwells among his people not because they have raised him a house but because they walk in his ways.
12:
The condition of the promise stands at the front of the verse, and the witnesses weigh both its grammar and its grace. Keil and Delitzsch note how the Hebrew throws 'the house which thou art building' into an absolute position at the head — 'As for the house which thou art building, if thou walkest in my statutes... I will set up my word, which I spake to thy father David.' The reference, they insist, is to 2 Samuel 7:12 and the everlasting establishment of David's throne; and the promise that follows in the next verse, that God will dwell in Israel's midst, is 'not a second promise added' but 'a special application' of that same Davidic pledge to the temple now being reared.

Gill keeps to the plain force of the opening clause: 'concerning this house which thou art in building,' these things are to be said as from the Lord — the whole message bearing directly on the fate of the very structure Solomon has in hand. The point both witnesses hold is that the magnificent building has been made expressly conditional. Its permanence is not secured by its stones, however costly, but by the obedience of the king and the people who worship in it.

The verse repays the reflection the witnesses give it because it fixes the theology of the whole chapter. The temple is not a talisman. God binds his continued presence and the standing of the dynasty to a walking 'in my statutes,' an executing of 'my judgments,' a keeping of 'all my commandments' — three phrases piled up to leave no room for a merely ceremonial religion that would trust the house while neglecting the law. The promise reaches back to David and forward to the whole subsequent history of the monarchy, for on these terms the throne would be established or, in default of them, the glory would depart. Keil's care to tie the words to 2 Samuel 7:12 and to David's own charge in 1 Kings 2:3 shows that the covenant is one continuous thing, renewed at each great juncture rather than multiplied into separate bargains. And Gill's insistence that the word concerns 'this house' keeps the abstraction earthed: it is precisely as Solomon lays cedar upon stone and gold upon cedar that he is told the building will only be what its builders' hearts make it. The witnesses do not divide; between the grammarian's absolute construction and the plain expositor's paraphrase they set out one truth — that obedience, not architecture, is the condition of God's abiding — and so prepare for the gracious declaration of the verse that follows.
13:
The promise reaches its height: God will dwell among the children of Israel and not forsake them. The witnesses read the pledge as the crown of the conditional word just given, and they are careful to say both what it grants and where it belongs in the larger covenant. Gill anchors the dwelling in the very house being built — 'in this house now building, and which was in the middle of the land, and where he would meet with them, and accept their sacrifices, not only of slain beasts, but of prayer and praise.' The temple thus becomes the appointed place of meeting, the centre to which the tribes would come and from which the divine presence would radiate.

Keil and Delitzsch guard the promise from being read as a fresh covenant. 'I will dwell in the midst of the children of Israel,' they explain, 'does not contain a second promise added to the one given in 2 Samuel 7:12, but simply a special application of it to the building of the temple.' The eternal establishment of David's throne 'involved' this indwelling, for a God enthroned forever among his people must have a place of habitation among them. They read the closing clause, 'I will not forsake my people Israel,' as the negative side of the same grace — the pledge of a presence that will not be withdrawn so long as the covenant is kept.

The witnesses do not contend, and the verse leaves little room for dispute; its whole force is doctrinal and consoling. What Gill supplies concretely — the house in the midst of the land, the place of sacrifice and of prayer — Keil supplies covenantally, tracing the indwelling back to the Davidic promise and forward to the assurance of an unfailing presence. Between them they establish the theological centre of the passage: the temple matters not as a monument but as the token that the living God has chosen to dwell with men. Yet neither lets the reader forget the condition attached a verse earlier. The dwelling is promised, and it is promised freely; but it is promised to a people summoned to walk in God's statutes, so that the glory which now fills the house may not one day rise and depart. The tension the chapter holds — lavish grace bound to real obedience — reaches its sharpest and sweetest point here, where the Maker of heaven and earth stoops to say that he will make his home in the midst of Israel and will not forsake them. It is the promise that gives the gold and the cedar their meaning, and it will echo forward to the greater dwelling of God with man that the whole structure was raised to foreshadow.
14:
A single, unadorned sentence resumes the narrative — 'so Solomon built the house, and finished it' — and with only one witness in view the synthesis draws its substance from that one voice, the verse's own words, and the flow of the chapter. Gill reads the verb 'finished' as the deliberate mark of completion: Solomon 'went on with it until he had completed it, animated and encouraged by the promise of the divine Presence,' provided due regard were had to the will and ways of God. The building is not merely continued but carried through to its end, and Gill attaches the perseverance directly to the word that had just come.

He then reports Abarbanel's reading of the divine message's timing and effect. The word, on this view, 'was sent him in the midst of his work, to strengthen and caution him from a dependence on the continuance of the fabric, because of the strength of it' — lest Solomon should trust the sheer solidity of the stone and cedar he had raised, when in truth 'its perpetuity depended on his, his successors', and the people's conduct and behaviour.' Yet the caution did not paralyse him. 'Conscious to himself of his own integrity, and hoping well of posterity, he was not discouraged, but proceeded in his undertaking until he had brought it to perfection.'

The verse, brief as it is, marks a real turning-point, and Gill's comment brings out its two forces. First, it is a resumption: the account of the building, broken off at the tenth verse for the promise and its condition, is here taken up again, the little clause 'so Solomon built the house' stitching the narrative back together and signalling that the interior work of gold and carved cedar is about to be described. Second, it is a testimony to obedient constancy. The condition attached to the promise — that the house's standing hung on faithfulness, not on its own strength — might have discouraged a lesser builder; instead it steadied Solomon to press on, as Matthew Henry observed of this same stretch, since 'the strictness of God's government will never drive a good man from his service, but quicken him in it.' Solomon 'built and finished,' and God 'went along with him till it was completed.' The synthesis of so slender a verse must be honest about its slenderness, but the honest drawing-out is plain enough: a man encouraged by promise and sobered by condition does not falter but finishes, and the finishing of the house, twice noted in this chapter, becomes a quiet emblem of the perseverance that carries God's work through to its perfection.
15:
With the interior lining the witnesses reach one of the chapter's richest verses, and the puritan Matthew Henry seizes it for a sustained meditation on what the whole temple typified. First the plain sense: the walls were wainscotted with cedar — 'strong and durable, and of a very sweet smell' — and the floor laid with cypress, so that, as the verse and its neighbours insist, no stone was seen within; the house was clothed entirely in wood, and that wood in time overlaid with gold, until, in Henry's words, 'gold was under foot there, as it should be in all the living temples.'

The grammatical-historical commentators tend the text. Keil and Delitzsch find the phrase 'walls of the ceiling' so striking that they judge the word a copyist's slip for 'beams of the ceiling'; they insist the whole inside was covered with wood 'so that nothing was to be seen of the stone wall,' while denying that the outer walls were ever cedar-clad. Ellicott prefers the marginal reading, agreeing with the Septuagint and Vulgate: 'from the floor to the walls of the ceiling he covered all with cedar, and laid the floor with planks of cypress.' Gill simply marks the stage — as yet only the outer stone walls stood, and now Solomon 'wainscotted it with cedar boards.'

But it is Henry who lifts the verse into its fourfold type, and lands the passage where the witnesses together aim. First, Christ is the true temple, who spoke of the temple of his body (John 2:21) and in whom 'dwelt the fulness of the Godhead, as the Shechinah in the temple'; through him 'we have access with confidence to God.' Second, every believer is a living temple in whom the Spirit dwells (1 Corinthians 3:16; 1 Corinthians 6:19), 'built upon Christ as its foundation and perfected in due time.' Third, the gospel church is the mystical temple, growing 'to a holy temple in the Lord' (Ephesians 2:21), where — unlike the tabernacle, which only Jews built — Gentiles too are 'built up a habitation of God' (Ephesians 2:22), the wider door of the temple and the narrower door of the oracle picturing the visible and invisible church. Fourth, heaven is the everlasting temple, its streets of pure gold (Revelation 21:21), where the church is fixed and no longer movable, and where every stone must be 'hewn and squared by divine grace' in this present state of preparation. The grammatical labourers keep the letter exact; Henry shows what the letter was for — and closes with the appeal that sinners come to Jesus, the living Foundation, to be built on him a part of this spiritual house.
16:
The verse partitions off the innermost room, and the witnesses' interest gathers, as so often in this chapter, on a single word — 'oracle' — and the disputed sense behind it. Keil and Delitzsch lay the whole weight here. The Hebrew term, defined in apposition as 'the most holy place,' is rendered by Aquila and Symmachus as a place of divine response, by Jerome as a speaking-place, and in the Vulgate as oraculum, all deriving it from the root 'to speak,' so that the word would mean 'the place where the speaking takes place' — where God gave answer to his people. Keil records that many modern commentators derive it otherwise, but he sets out the traditional etymology with evident sympathy, for it fits the character of the shrine as the seat of the divine voice.

The witnesses agree on the plain construction: Solomon built off 'twenty cubits on the sides' — that is, a space twenty cubits deep at the hinder end of the house — and lined it, floor and walls, with cedar, to make within the temple a separate inner room, the Holy of Holies. Gill fixes the arrangement clearly: 'he built the most holy place, which was twenty cubits long, at the end of the holy place, which he wainscotted as the other.' The innermost sanctuary is thus carved out of the eastern-to-western length of the house, a perfect inner chamber prepared to receive the ark.

(A note attached to this verse from an account of Saul among the prophets belongs to another book entirely and has no bearing on the temple; the witnesses proper to the passage are Gill and Keil.)

The verse's importance lies in what it prepares. By walling off the twenty-cubit cube at the far end — the room that the following verses will show to be gold-covered and windowless, guarded by golden chains and folding doors of olive-wood — Solomon reproduces and enlarges the tabernacle's Most Holy Place, the dwelling of the divine presence over the ark. Keil's patient inquiry into whether the room is named for its silence or for the speaking that issued from it is not idle; it touches the very meaning of the sanctuary. If 'oracle' be the right rendering, then the darkest and most secret chamber of the house is precisely the place from which God made himself heard — his voice sounding from the innermost seclusion, his answer given from behind the veil. The most holy place is thus both the most hidden and the most communicative point of the whole structure, and the witnesses, in fixing its dimensions and probing its name, quietly establish the theological heart of the temple toward which every measure of gold and cedar now moves.
17:
The verse states the length of the Holy Place — forty cubits — and the witnesses, with only the plain measure before them, use the occasion to set the two rooms of the house in proportion and to look ahead to the fittings. Keil and Delitzsch read the notice forward, gathering it with the verses that follow: the temple proper 'before it,' that is, in front of the oracle, was forty cubits long, so that the sixty-cubit house divided into a forty-cubit Holy Place and a twenty-cubit Most Holy Place. They pass at once to the adornment the next verses record — the cedar 'carved with knops and open flowers,' all cedar so that 'there was no stone seen,' and the preparing of the oracle within 'to set there the ark of the covenant of the LORD' — treating the length as the threshold of the whole interior description.

Gill keeps to the single point, glossing 'the house, that is, the temple before it' as 'the holy place, which was before the most holy of holies.' His concern is simply to make plain which of the two rooms is meant: not the innermost shrine just partitioned off, but the larger outer sanctuary that lay before it, where the priests ministered at the golden altar, the table, and the lampstands.

There is nothing here for the witnesses to dispute, and the synthesis must be honest that the verse is a measurement and little more. Yet the measurement is not without meaning. The forty-and-twenty division fixes the ratio of the two holy rooms at two to one, the Holy Place double the depth of the Most Holy, just as the whole house doubled the dimensions of the tabernacle it copied. The larger room was the place of daily priestly service, open to the ministering priests; the smaller, the windowless cube of the divine presence, entered but once a year. By stating the forty cubits of the first, the text lets the reader complete the arithmetic and see the sixty-cubit house resolve into its two sacred spaces. Keil's habit of reading straight on into the carved cedar and the waiting ark is itself instructive: the bare number is given not for its own sake but as the frame within which all the glory to come — the knops and flowers, the gold, the cherubim, the folding doors — will be set. The Holy Place is measured so that it may be furnished, and the witnesses, having fixed its length, turn with the text toward the ornament and the overlaying that will make the plain cedar shell a house fit for the name of the Lord.
18:
With a single short clause — 'the cedar of the house within was carved with knops and open flowers; all was cedar, there was no stone seen' — the text records the ornament of the interior, and here only one witness speaks to it directly. Gill, commenting on 'the cedar of the house within,' notes simply that it was the wood 'with which the inside of the place was lined.' The synthesis of so spare a verse must draw its substance from that one plain observation, from the verse's own vivid words, and from the setting the surrounding account supplies.

What the verse establishes is the completeness of the cedar covering and the character of its carving. The knops — rounded ornaments, likened to gourds or eggs — and the open flowers, blossoms carved in full bloom, ran over the wainscot, turning the whole inner surface into a garden worked in fragrant wood. And the clause 'all was cedar; there was no stone seen' seals the point the chapter has been quietly making from the walls of unheard iron onward: within the house, the plain masonry vanished entirely beneath a skin of carved and living wood, soon to be overlaid with gold. Nothing rough, nothing merely structural, met the eye of one who entered; every visible surface was wrought and adorned.

The devotional resonance the other witnesses draw elsewhere from this ornament — the palm trees and open flowers that recur on the walls and doors, read as figures of the upright and of the graces of the Spirit flourishing in the house of God — belongs properly to the verses that repeat these carvings, and there the reflection will be gathered. Here the text is content to announce the motifs and their totality. Yet even in its brevity the verse carries the theology of the sanctuary's interior. The garden imagery — buds and blossoms and fruit-like knops covering the walls of the place where God met his people — recalls the lost garden and anticipates the restored one, the house of God appointed to be a paradise regained, adorned with the beauty of holiness. That the stone was wholly hidden speaks, too, of a glory not native to the bare material but conferred upon it: the plain quarried rock, dressed in silence and then clothed in carved cedar and gold, becomes a parable of the natural made beautiful by grace. The witnesses proper to the verse are few and their comment slight, but the verse's own words, set within the movement of the chapter, say more than the single gloss upon them — that within the sanctuary all was made lovely, and nothing of the common was left in sight.
19:
The verse turns to the innermost purpose of the whole house: 'the oracle he prepared in the house within, to set there the ark of the covenant of the LORD.' The witnesses read it as the disclosure of why the temple exists at all — to give the ark a resting-place and to make a seat for the divine voice. Gill draws out the sense of 'oracle' that governs the passage: 'in the innermost part of the house he fitted up the oracle, or speaking place, from whence the Lord spake and gave answer, to receive what was to be put into it' — namely, the ark of the covenant. The innermost room is thus defined by its two functions at once, as the place that holds the ark and the place from which God speaks.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke, on this verse, declines to expand the picture piecemeal and refers the reader instead to the full description of the temple appended at the chapter's end — a characteristic caution, for Clarke had already confessed his unwillingness to reconstruct the building's precise appearance where the ancient authorities disagree. His restraint is itself a comment: the meaning of the oracle does not depend on settling every architectural nicety, but on the plain fact the verse asserts — that here, in the prepared inner room, the ark was to be set.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse's weight is theological rather than technical. The whole vast labour of the chapter — the doubled dimensions, the silent stones, the tiered chambers, the cedar and the gold — finds its reason here: all of it is preparation for the ark, and the ark is the token of the covenant and the place of meeting between God and Israel. Gill's linking of the two ideas, the receptacle of the ark and the source of the divine answer, catches the genius of the sanctuary. The most secret chamber of the house is not a mere treasury but a place of speech; from behind the veil, above the mercy-seat and between the cherubim, the Lord had promised to commune with his people. So the verse gathers up the purpose of everything built so far and points to everything still to come — the overlaying of this room in pure gold, the great cherubim to be set within it, the folding doors of olive-wood to guard it. The temple is being made ready, in its inmost part, for the presence and the voice of God; and the witnesses, brief as their notes are here, rightly read the verse as the hinge on which the sanctuary's meaning turns.
20:
The dimensions of the oracle — twenty cubits each way, a perfect cube — and its overlaying with pure gold occupy the witnesses, who divide their labour between the geometry, the philology, and a puzzling phrase about the altar. Keil and Delitzsch render the opening literally, 'the interior of the hinder room was twenty cubits the length, twenty the breadth, and twenty its height,' agreeing with Kimchi that the difficult word means 'the inner part' of the room. On the gold they linger over the Hebrew for 'fine' or 'pure' gold, canvassing whether the term derives from a root meaning 'to shut up' — gold carefully preserved — or 'to select,' gold chosen and refined, and leaving the derivation open while the sense stands: the costliest gold sheathed the most holy place.

Ellicott corrects the English of the opening clause. 'In the forepart,' though a literal rendering, misleads; the sense is 'in the inner part,' the wall facing the entrance and thence the region behind it — the Most Holy Place itself. He also untangles the notice about the altar: the translators, he says, 'have been misled by the context to anticipate' the gilding described below, when the meaning here is that Solomon 'covered the altar,' presumably of stone, 'with cedar.' Gill, characteristically practical, reads the phrase differently, taking 'the forepart' as the front of the oracle, lower than its rear so as to let in the light of the lampstands and the smoke of the incense from the Holy Place — a reconstruction the grammatical-historical readers do not follow.

Beneath these differences of construction the witnesses agree on the substance: the innermost shrine was a cube of twenty cubits, walled and floored and roofed in gold of the purest kind, with a cedar altar — the altar of incense — associated with it. The perfect cubic form is not accidental; it will reappear at the end of the Bible in the cube of the new Jerusalem, and the witnesses' care to establish the equal dimensions preserves a shape freighted with meaning. The gold, too, is more than magnificence: the room from which God spoke and where the ark rested was clothed throughout in the most precious metal known, so that the eye met nothing common in the place of the divine presence. Where Gill and Ellicott part company over the meaning of 'the forepart' — whether it names a lower front section of the shrine or simply the inner region facing the entrance — the disagreement touches only the arrangement of light and smoke, not the room's holiness or its splendour. The witnesses leave the reader with a golden cube prepared for the ark and the voice of God, its every surface gleaming, its cedar altar ready for the incense of prayer that would rise before the veil.
21:
The overlaying of the Holy Place with gold, and a curious 'partition by the chains of gold' before the oracle, draw the witnesses into equal parts arithmetic and puzzlement. Gill supplies the staggering reckoning: the gold that sheathed the inner house 'amounted to six hundred talents,' which he converts to 'seven million three hundred and thirty-two thousand ducats of gold, or three million forty-five thousand pounds sterling' — a sum meant to leave the reader breathless at the cost lavished on the sanctuary.

The obscure clause about the golden chains occupies the grammatical-historical commentators, and they confess its difficulty. Keil and Delitzsch report that the words are 'very obscure'; against their own earlier view that the partition was the wall between Holy and Most Holy, they now take the house here to mean the Holy Place as distinguished from the oracle, and follow Gesenius and the older commentators in reading the verb 'in the sense of bolting or fastening' — Solomon 'bolted, or fastened, with golden chains' the entrance before the inner room. Ellicott agrees that the phrase 'is difficult,' notes that the Septuagint and Vulgate read it quite differently, but judges the English 'substantially correct,' and offers the likeliest sense: Solomon 'made a golden chain to go across the entrance in the partition before the oracle, in front of the veil, so as to be an additional guard against intrusion.'

Between them the witnesses converge on a picture even where the words resist them: the Holy Place gleaming with gold at fabulous expense, and the way into the Most Holy Place barred not only by its folding doors and veil but by a further screen or chain of gold, a token that the innermost room was set apart under the strictest guard. The theology is quietly present in the very difficulty. That the entrance to the oracle should be so jealously secured — doors, veil, and golden chain together — speaks of the awful separation the old covenant maintained between a holy God and a sinful people; the way into the holiest 'was not yet made manifest,' as the epistle to the Hebrews would later teach, while the first tabernacle yet had standing. The witnesses do not resolve every crux of the Hebrew, and they are honest that they cannot; Keil even reverses a former opinion, and Ellicott admits the ancient versions diverge. But their shared verdict is clear enough: gold beyond calculation covered the sanctuary, and the door to the place of the divine presence was chained and screened, magnificence and inaccessibility set side by side, the glory of the room proclaiming the greatness of the God who dwelt there and the barrier before it proclaiming that he could not yet be approached.
22:
The summary that 'the whole house he overlaid with gold, until he had finished all the house' is met by the witnesses with a plain acknowledgement of its excess, and with one finely-drawn theological note about the incense-altar. Adam Clarke speaks for the general astonishment: 'it is impossible to calculate this expense, or the quantity of gold employed in this sacred building.' Where Gill had ventured a figure for the oracle's gold, Clarke simply owns that the total defeats reckoning — the whole interior, both the Holy Place and the Most Holy, sheathed alike in gold.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott takes up the phrase about 'the whole altar that was by the oracle' and turns it into a small but illuminating study. This is the altar of incense, which, though it stood 'before the veil' and therefore physically in the Holy Place (as Exodus 30:6 and Exodus 40:26 require), was 'considered to belong in idea rather to the Holy of Holies.' The reason is liturgical: the incense offered on it 'signified the approach by worship to the unseen presence of God,' and the taking of its censer was the condition for entering the oracle on the great Day of Atonement. Hence, he notes, Exodus 40:5 can speak of the altar as 'set before the ark of the testimony,' and our verse as belonging 'to the oracle.' Ellicott adds the exegetical prize: this, 'probably, is the explanation of the well-known passage in the Epistle to the Hebrews' (Hebrews 9:4), where the Holiest is said to have 'had the altar of incense' — wrongly rendered 'censer' in the Authorised Version. Keil and Gill confirm the plain sense, that both rooms were gilded and 'the whole altar that was by the oracle' overlaid with gold.

The witnesses do not divide; they complement one another, Clarke voicing the reverent wonder at the incalculable cost and Ellicott resolving the odd phrase into a genuine insight. Together they leave two impressions. First, the sheer, unmeasurable lavishness: the finishing of the house meant that not a surface of the interior remained ungilded, gold poured out until the work was done. Second, the theological knot the incense-altar ties between the two holy rooms. Standing in the outer sanctuary yet reckoned to the inner, it embodied the worshipper's approach to the God who dwelt behind the veil, the fragrant smoke of prayer rising toward a presence still unseen. Ellicott's linking of the detail to the disputed word in Hebrews shows how a builder's inventory can throw light on the New Testament's reading of the old sanctuary — the altar 'by the oracle' pointing forward to the prayers of the saints and the one High Priest who would pass, censer in hand, into the true Most Holy Place.
23:
The two great cherubim of olive-wood, each ten cubits high, set within the oracle, draw from Ellicott the longest and most searching note in the chapter, and from Gill a characteristically typological reading. Ellicott begins with the plain comparison: these figures 'were copied from the Tabernacle,' but enlarged, and made of olive-wood overlaid with gold rather than solid gold. He marks a likely change of posture. In Exodus 25:18–20 the cherubim faced the mercy-seat, covering it with their wings; here, from the careful description of wings outstretched to touch the walls and meet in the midst, 'it would seem that they must have been turned so as to face the entrance.'

Ellicott then ranges across the whole biblical witness. The cherubim are described in only a few places — the passages in Exodus 37:7–9, this chapter, the parallel in 2 Chronicles 3:10–13, and the visions of Ezekiel 1:4–25 and Ezekiel 10:1–22 that shaped the imagery of the Apocalypse — and their form is nowhere plainly given. Josephus confessed that 'no one can tell, or even conjecture, of what shape the cherubim were,' the tradition having been lost in the Second Temple, where there was no ark. But whatever their form, Ellicott insists 'they were in no sense representations or emblems of Deity,' unlike the winged figures of Assyria or Egypt; they 'symbolise the great physical forces of the universe, as guided by superhuman angelic intelligence to serve the supreme will of God' — set to guard the tree of life in Genesis 3:24, bearing the throne in Ezekiel 1:10, joining the worship of the redeemed in Revelation 4:6–8. He even suggests that the changed posture 'denoted a change of idea': the old attitude one of worship of God, 'the new rather of manifestation of His glory to man.'

Gill reads the pair devotionally and offers two figures. They may emblem 'the angels in their greatness and glory, who are always in the presence of God, behold his face, and wait to do his will'; or, more boldly, 'the two witnesses,' the two olive trees standing before the God of the whole earth in Revelation 11:4, who 'have boldness to enter into the holiest of all' and knowledge of the mysteries of the ark and mercy-seat. The witnesses do not so much debate as work at different depths: Ellicott the historian and theologian of symbol, tracing the cherubim from Eden to the Apocalypse and refusing every pagan comparison; Gill the preacher, hearing in the golden guardians of the shrine a figure of the worshipping angels or the bold witnesses of Christ. Both agree that these towering forms, made expressly for the darkness of the oracle, proclaimed the God of Israel Lord over all creation, hymned in the seraphic 'Holy, holy, holy' of Isaiah 6:3.
24:
The verse begins the exact measuring of the cherubs' wings — five cubits for the one wing, five for the other, ten from tip to tip — and the witnesses treat it as one continuous piece of surveying, the grammatical-historical Keil reading straight on through the four verses that give the figures' full dimensions and placing. Keil and Delitzsch gather the measures together: each wing five cubits, so that the outspread pair of one cherub reached ten cubits across; the second cherub 'of one measure and one size' with the first; the height of each ten cubits; and, when set within the inner house, the wings so extended 'that the wing of the one touched the one wall, and the wing of the other cherub touched the other wall, and their wings touched one another in the midst of the house.' The two ten-cubit figures, standing side by side with wings outstretched, exactly spanned the twenty-cubit breadth of the oracle, wingtip to wall on either hand and wingtip to wingtip at the centre.

Gill keeps to the single clause before him, glossing 'the one wing of the cherub' as its span 'when stretched out on one side.' His brevity is proper to the verse, which does no more than begin the tally; the fuller picture waits on the verses that complete it.

There is no dispute here, and the synthesis must be candid that the verse is pure measurement. Yet the measurement is eloquent in its own way. That the two cherubim, each as tall as the room was deep in its cube, should stretch their wings to fill the entire width of the Most Holy Place — a continuous canopy of gold-covered olive-wood from wall to wall — gave the innermost shrine an overshadowing majesty. Beneath that outstretched span the ark of the covenant was to stand, so that the mercy-seat lay under the shadow of these great wings as it had lain under the smaller wings of the golden cherubim Moses made. The careful equality Keil underscores — both cherubs of one measure, one size, one height — bespeaks the ordered symmetry of the whole design, nothing left to chance in the place of the divine presence. The witnesses, in patiently checking the arithmetic, are not merely antiquarians; they are letting the reader see how precisely the figures were fitted to the room, so that the wings should meet exactly at the centre and touch exactly at the walls. The effect was a chamber wholly overshadowed, its every dimension answered by the span of the guardian cherubim, prepared to receive the ark beneath a golden heaven of wings.
25:
This brief verse repeats what the last began — 'the other cherub was ten cubits; both the cherubims were of one measure and one size' — and with only Gill commenting directly, the synthesis draws on that single gloss, the verse's own insistence on equality, and the setting the surrounding measures supply. Gill reads the ten cubits of 'the other cherub' as its wingspan: 'that is, its wings stretched out measured so many cubits, as did those of the other.' The verse's whole point, on his reading, is the matching of the second figure to the first, wing for wing and cubit for cubit.

The repetition is not idle. Hebrew narrative often doubles a statement to fix it firmly, and the text is at pains to establish that the two cherubim were exactly alike — 'of one measure and one size' — so that neither overtopped nor outreached the other, and the pair stood as a perfectly balanced whole across the breadth of the oracle. Where a lesser account might have described one figure and left the second to be assumed, the record insists on the equality expressly, twice over, lest any imagine the guardians of the ark to have been unequal or ill-matched. The symmetry mattered because the room mattered: the Most Holy Place, the seat of the divine presence, was fashioned throughout with an exact and deliberate order, its two great overshadowing forms answering one another in every dimension.

There is nothing here for the witnesses to debate, and honesty requires the synthesis to be short where the verse is short and the comment slight. But the verse's very brevity, drawn out, yields its lesson. The pair of cherubim, each ten cubits tall and each with wings spanning ten cubits, together filled the twenty-cubit width of the shrine — a fact the equality of this verse makes possible, for only if both were of one measure could their wings meet precisely at the centre and touch precisely at the walls, as the next verse and its neighbours describe. The insistence on identical size is thus the quiet hinge on which the whole arrangement turns. And there is a fitness in it beyond the mechanical: the two angelic figures, alike in stature and in glory, standing in unbroken symmetry over the ark, image the ordered and harmonious worship of heaven, where the servants of God stand before his face in a concord that nothing mars. Gill's single sentence catches the verse's plain sense; the verse's own repetition supplies the emphasis; and the design it guards — a shrine of perfect proportion, prepared for the presence of the Lord — supplies the meaning.
26:
The height of each cherub — ten cubits — is stated a third time, and the witnesses note both the emphasis of the repetition and the sheer scale it records. Gill observes that the measure 'is repeated' from the earlier notice 'that it might be observed, being of an extraordinary size.' The reiteration, on his reading, is deliberate: the text lingers on the ten cubits because the figures were so remarkably large, and it will not let the reader pass over their vastness lightly.

Adam Clarke translates the cubits into a height the modern eye can grasp — 'the height of each cherub was about eighteen feet and three inches' — and refers the reader, for the form and nature of the cherubim, to his fuller note on Exodus 25:18. Between the two witnesses the point is plain: these were colossal figures, some eighteen feet tall, standing within a room only about thirty feet high, so that they towered over the ark and nearly reached the ceiling of the shrine.

There is no dispute here; the witnesses simply register the fact and its magnitude, and the synthesis, drawing out so brief a verse, must dwell on what that magnitude signified. The Most Holy Place was a cube of twenty cubits, and into it Solomon set two figures each ten cubits high with wings each ten cubits across — beings that filled the room in both breadth and height, an overshadowing presence of gold-covered olive-wood on a scale the tabernacle had never known. The enlargement was fitting. The old sanctuary had been a portable tent, its cherubim small and wrought upon the mercy-seat itself; the new house was a fixed temple of stone and cedar and gold, and its guardians grew to match the grandeur of the dwelling. Yet the effect was not display for its own sake but the impressing of awe. Anyone who could have looked within — and only the high priest, once a year, might — would have seen the ark dwarfed beneath these immense winged forms, a visible declaration of the majesty of the God whose throne they attended. Gill's insistence that the repeated measure marks 'an extraordinary size,' and Clarke's plain conversion into feet and inches, serve the same end: to make the reader feel the scale of the shrine's furnishings, and through the scale, the greatness of the One for whose presence the whole golden chamber, with its towering cherubim, was so carefully and so lavishly prepared.
27:
The setting of the cherubim within the inner house, their wings spread from wall to wall and meeting at the centre, is the moment the previous verses of measurement have been building toward, and here Gill is the one witness to comment directly. He fixes the place plainly: 'he set the cherubim within the inner house,' which he identifies as 'the holy of holies.' The colossal figures, measured and matched in the foregoing verses, are now installed in the one room for which they were made — the windowless golden cube of the divine presence.

The verse itself supplies the picture that Gill's brief gloss assumes and that Keil, reading these verses together, had already drawn out: the two cherubim stood side by side with wings outstretched, so that the wing of the one touched the one wall and the wing of the other touched the opposite wall, while their inner wings met in the midst of the house. Two ten-cubit spans, wingtip to wingtip across the centre, exactly filled the twenty-cubit breadth of the oracle, and the ark of the covenant was to rest beneath them. The arrangement completes the shrine: the room prepared, the walls and floor gilded, the altar of incense set before it, and now the guardian figures fixed in their place, a canopy of golden wings overshadowing the seat of God's presence.

There is no controversy here, and the verse, plain as it is, marks a genuine climax in the chapter's long architectural account. Everything within the oracle now stands ready for the ark that 1 Kings 8:6 will bring in and set 'under the wings of the cherubims' — the very posture this verse establishes. The theological weight lies in that overshadowing. As the smaller cherubim Moses made had stretched their wings over the mercy-seat, so these greater ones spread theirs over the whole room, making of the Most Holy Place a chamber wholly canopied by the emblems of angelic attendance. The God of Israel would dwell here 'between the cherubim,' enthroned above the ark beneath the shadow of their wings, and the careful placing this verse records — each wing reaching precisely to its wall, the pair meeting precisely at the centre — gave visible form to that enthronement. Gill's single sentence names the room; the verse names the posture; and together they show the sanctuary's heart made ready, the towering figures locked into the exact places the measurements had prepared for them, awaiting only the ark and the glory that would fill the house.
28:
The overlaying of the cherubim with gold is recorded in a single clause, and Gill alone comments, turning the plain fact toward its spiritual sense. The gilding of the great figures, he suggests, 'may denote the purity and excellency of angels; or the precious gifts and graces of the Spirit, with which the witnesses of Christ are adorned.' Having already read the two cherubim as emblems either of the attending angels or of the two witnesses of the Apocalypse, Gill carries the same double reading forward: if the figures picture the angels, their gold speaks of angelic purity and excellency; if they picture the faithful witnesses, their gold speaks of the graces of the Spirit that adorn those who testify for Christ.

The synthesis of so brief a verse must be honest about its slenderness and draw its substance from the one witness, the verse's own words, and the movement of the chapter. What the clause adds to the picture already built is completeness of splendour. The cherubim were carved of olive-wood, a firm and lasting timber; but bare wood, however finely wrought, would not have suited the golden shrine in which they stood. Every other visible surface of the Most Holy Place — walls, floor, altar — had been sheathed in gold; the guardian figures could be no exception, and so they too were overlaid, until the whole room, its walls and its towering occupants alike, gleamed with one uniform glory. Nothing of the common wood was left in sight, just as, within the Holy Place, no stone was seen beneath the cedar. The principle that has governed the interior throughout — that in the place of the divine presence all should be precious, all should be beautiful, nothing merely structural or plain permitted to the eye — is here carried to its last detail.

Gill's devotional gloss, offered without insistence, gives the verse a voice beyond the inventory. The gold upon the cherubim becomes a figure of adornment by grace: the servants of God, whether the sinless angels or the redeemed witnesses, are not left in their native state but are clothed with an excellency conferred upon them, made to shine with a glory not their own. It is a fitting reflection for the close of the shrine's furnishing. The room made ready for the presence of God is a place where everything has been transfigured — wood into gold, the plain into the glorious — and the two great figures that overshadow the ark, themselves gilded from head to wing, stand as the crowning instance of that transfiguration, an emblem of the beauty with which God adorns those who serve before his face.
29:
The carving of cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers upon all the walls of the house draws the reformed Gill to a devotional reading of the motifs and the grammatical-historical Ellicott to a question of extent. Gill, having taken the cherubim as figures of the angels, reads the palm trees as fitly representing 'just and upright men,' who flourish 'both in the church of God below, and in heaven above'; and he finds in the open, full-blown flowers a picture of 'the open and full-blown flowers of divine grace,' displayed most perfectly in the heavenly state. The walls of the sanctuary thus become, on his reading, a wrought emblem of the whole company of the redeemed — angels and upright men together, grace come to full bloom — carved into the very fabric of the house of God.

Ellicott presses the plain sense and raises a nice point of arrangement. If we take 'he carved all the walls of the house round about' literally, then this carving of cherubim and palm trees, over and above the general decoration of gourds and open flowers, 'was spread over all the walls of the house.' He owns that one might have expected it 'confined to the oracle within, and to the partition without,' since the cherubim 'belonged especially to the oracle' — but the text, as it stands, extends the figures throughout both rooms. He does not force the question, but leaves the reader to weigh whether the whole interior or only its holiest parts bore the carved guardians and palms.

The two witnesses do not so much disagree as attend to different things — Gill to what the carvings mean, Ellicott to how far they reached — and together they enrich the picture of the interior. What had been announced earlier, that the cedar within was carved with knops and open flowers so that no stone was seen, is here completed with the addition of the cherubim and the palms, the whole wainscot turned into a carved and gilded garden peopled with winged guardians. The imagery is that of paradise: the palm, symbol of the righteous flourishing (as the Psalm has it, planted in the house of the Lord and flourishing in the courts of our God), the flowers in full bloom, the cherubim who kept the way to the tree of life. Solomon's craftsmen made of the sanctuary's walls a figure of Eden restored and of the church perfected, angels and upright men set among blossoming trees in the presence of God. Gill's reading draws out that meaning; Ellicott's caution keeps it tethered to the text's own words. Where the carving was spread — over all the walls, or chiefly in the oracle — may remain open; that it made the house a paradise in wood and gold the witnesses leave in no doubt.
30:
The overlaying of the floor with gold, 'within and without,' completes the gilding of the interior, and the witnesses read it both plainly and typologically. Keil and Delitzsch settle the sense of 'within and without': Solomon overlaid the floor with gold 'in the Most Holy Place and in the Holy Place also' — the two rooms of the house, the inner and the outer sanctuary, so that from the innermost cube to the larger room before it not even the ground underfoot was left ungilded. The whole interior, walls and floor together, now gleamed with one unbroken splendour of gold.

Gill takes the same fact and lifts it toward heaven. The golden floor recalls to him the street of the new Jerusalem, said to be 'pure gold' in Revelation 21:21; it 'expresses the purity and magnificence' of the sanctuary, and 'particularly the holy conversation of them that dwell in the church, in heaven,' signified by so precious a pavement. Where Matthew Henry had observed elsewhere in this chapter that 'gold was under foot' in the temple 'as it should be in all the living temples' — the abundance of it lessening its worth — Gill hears in the golden floor a figure of the sanctified walk of God's people, whose very manner of life, in the church below and the church above, answers to the preciousness of the ground on which they tread.

The witnesses do not divide; Keil fixes the extent of the gilding and Gill draws out its meaning, and the two together bring the overlaying of the house to its completion. What the clause records is the last surface conquered by gold. The stone had been hidden under cedar; the cedar carved and overlaid; the cherubim gilded; and now the floor itself, in both rooms, covered with the same metal, so that one who entered stood upon gold and was surrounded by gold on every side. The theological resonance the witnesses find is not strained. A house in which even the floor is precious teaches that in the dwelling of God nothing is common, and that those who walk there are called to a corresponding holiness of life — a golden conversation to match a golden floor. And the forward reach to the new Jerusalem, whose street is pure gold, sets the earthly sanctuary in its true light as a shadow of the heavenly: the gilded floor of Solomon's temple a faint anticipation of the city where the redeemed will walk on gold in the unveiled presence of God. The gilding finished, the chapter turns from surfaces to the doors that closed the holy rooms.
31:
The doors of the oracle, made of olive-wood, occupy the witnesses with a devotional figure from Gill and a knot of measurement from the grammatical-historical school. Gill reads the two-leaved folding door of the Most Holy Place as 'typical of Christ, the door into the church above and below, the way to heaven and eternal life, the true olive tree.' The choice of olive-wood, that is, is not indifferent to him: as Christ is the true olive, so the door of olive-wood that gave entrance to the holiest place figures the one Way by which men come to God — an echo of the Lord's own word that he is the door.

The grammatical-historical commentators labour over the obscure clause about 'the lintel and side posts,' or 'a fifth part of the wall.' Keil and Delitzsch confess the words 'obscure' and 'interpreted in very different ways,' but conclude that they 'can hardly have any other meaning' than that 'the projecting framework of the doors occupied the fifth part of the breadth of the wall.' They reject the rival rendering of Böttcher and Thenius, 'posts of fifth strength,' as unsupported. Ellicott reaches the same result and works it into feet: if the outside measure of lintel and post was a fifth of the twenty-cubit wall, that is four cubits, or six feet, so that each door 'would be something less than six feet by three' — a doorway of moderate size, since the folding doors of the oracle 'as a rule stood open, showing the veil and the golden chains.' He adds a nice observation on the gilding to come, that the gold was 'spread,' made to cover the carvings in relief, rather than merely fitted over them as in the outer doors.

The witnesses do not contend; Gill supplies the type and the grammatical-historical readers the dimensions, and together they present the entrance to the holiest as both meaningful and modest. The door was small, and commonly open, its office not to shut men out absolutely — the veil and the golden chain did that — but to frame the way into the presence of God. That it was wrought of olive-wood, carved with cherubim and palms and overlaid with gold, made it of a piece with the shrine it guarded; that it stood, as Ellicott notes, usually open before the veil, kept the way visibly in view even while the innermost access remained barred.
