Gill's figure gathers the whole into its gospel meaning: the olive-wood door of the oracle points to Christ the true olive and the true door, through whom alone the church on earth and in heaven has entrance to the Father. The measurements fix the fact; the type unfolds its purpose; and the reader is shown, in a doorway of gilded olive, a shadow of the one Way to eternal life.
32:
The two leaves of the olive-wood door, carved with cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers and overlaid with gold, are described with a precision the witnesses gladly follow, the grammatical-historical Keil dwelling on the manner of the gilding and Ellicott on the make of the doors. Keil and Delitzsch explain the striking phrase that Solomon 'spread gold upon the cherubims and upon the palm trees': he laid gold-leaf upon them, 'so that, as Rashi observes, all the figures, the elevations and depressions of the carved work, were impressed upon the coating of gold-leaf, and were thus plainly seen.' The gold did not bury the carving but took its shape, every raised cherub and incised palm showing through the beaten metal. Keil weighs, and finally doubts, Thenius's inference that the gilding was therefore confined to the figures alone rather than covering the whole door.

Ellicott attends to the doors as objects. He distinguishes them from the doors of the outer sanctuary — 'those into the Holy place from the porch, of cypress wood, were naturally made larger' — and reckons the height of those larger doors at five or seven and a half cubits, according to how the wall is measured. Of the olive-wood doors here he notes the construction that made such heavy leaves workable: since they would be 'much heavier, and more frequently opened and shut,' each leaf 'was made to fold again upon itself,' a folding door within a folding door, so that the great valves might swing without being unwieldy. Gill keeps to the plain notice, observing that 'the two leaves of the door' are named again 'for the sake of the ornament of them,' which the verse proceeds to describe.

The witnesses do not divide; they distribute the labour, Keil on the gilding, Ellicott on the joinery, Gill on the reason for the repetition, and together they complete the picture of the oracle's entrance. What emerges is a door of great richness and careful craft: two leaves of olive-wood, each hinged to fold upon itself for ease of movement, carved throughout with the same paradisal imagery that ran over the walls — the winged guardians, the flourishing palms, the open flowers — and then covered in gold-leaf beaten so closely to the carving that every figure stood out through the metal. The effect married beauty to reverence. The way into the holiest was closed by no plain barrier but by a wrought and gilded gate, its carved cherubim continuing at the threshold the very forms that overshadowed the ark within, so that the door itself preached the holiness of the place it guarded. Keil's care over the gold-leaf that took the shape of the carving is not mere technique; it shows how the craftsmen contrived that even the gilding should reveal rather than conceal the sacred figures, the glory of the gold and the meaning of the carving displayed together upon the door of the presence of God.
33:
The doors of the Holy Place — the entrance from the porch into the outer sanctuary — are described in their turn, and the grammatical-historical commentators fasten on the proportion of their framework and the choice of their wood. Keil and Delitzsch read the difficult clause to mean that Solomon 'made upon the door of the Holy Place posts of olive-wood from a fourth' — a framework that 'occupied a fourth of the breadth of the wall,' or five cubits broad, wider than the fifth-part framing of the oracle's door. The doors themselves, they note, were of cypress rather than olive, 'each of the folding doors consisting of two leaves, each of which was made to turn by itself, so that it could be opened and shut alone.' They venture a reason for the change of timber: cypress was chosen for the Holy Place 'probably because it is lighter in weight, and therefore less likely to sink' under the strain of larger and more frequently used doors.

Gill supplies only the identification, glossing 'the door of the temple' as the entrance of 'the holy place,' and so distinguishing it clearly from the door of the oracle just described. Between the two witnesses the arrangement is plain: the outer doorway, broader in its framing and hung with lighter cypress leaves, gave the priests their daily access to the Holy Place, while the inner doorway, narrower and of olive-wood, guarded the once-a-year approach to the Most Holy.

There is no dispute here; the interest is entirely in the graded distinction between the two entrances, and the witnesses draw it out faithfully. The difference of framework — a fourth of the wall for the outer door, a fifth for the inner — and the difference of wood — cypress without, olive within — mark the ascending holiness of the two rooms even in the carpentry of their doors. The outer door was the larger and the more used, opened continually by the ministering priests; the inner the smaller and the more sacred, opened but seldom and guarded besides by veil and golden chain. Keil's practical note about the lightness of cypress is characteristic of the grammatical-historical school's attention to the how as well as the what: the builders chose their materials with an eye to weight and wear, the heavier-worked outer doors given a timber less apt to sag. Yet the practical and the symbolic run together. Even the choice of wood observed the gradations of the sanctuary, and the reader who follows the witnesses through these fine distinctions is led, door by door, deeper into the house — from the porch through the cypress doors into the Holy Place, and from there, past the olive-wood doors, toward the golden cube where the presence of God dwelt between the cherubim.
34:
The two folding leaves of the outer doors, each divided so as to turn upon itself, are recorded here, and the grammatical-historical Keil reads the notice forward into the carving, the gilding, and the courts that follow. Keil and Delitzsch take the verse's plain statement — 'the two leaves of the one door were folding, and the two leaves of the other door were folding' — as describing cypress doors each made of leaves that folded again upon themselves, and then pass to the next verses: the carving of 'cherubims and palm trees and open flowers,' and the gilding in which 'the gold was levelled or smoothed over that which had been engraved,' beaten thin and laid so as to fit closely to the figures. They add the technical note that ancient gilding was generally 'effected by the laying on of gold plate, which was fastened with tacks,' citing 2 Chronicles 3:9.

Gill keeps to the immediate point, observing that 'the posts were of olive, but the doors of fir,' and that these too 'were both folding doors, as appears by what follows.' The distinction he preserves — olive-wood posts framing cypress (fir) leaves — matches the arrangement Keil describes, and confirms that the outer entrance combined the more precious wood in its framework with the lighter timber in its swinging leaves.

(A note appended to this verse concerning the choosing of Saul as king belongs to another book altogether and throws no light on the temple doors; the witnesses proper to the passage are Gill and Keil.)

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse itself is a matter of joinery, yet the joinery is not without its lesson. The elaborate folding of the leaves — each valve made of parts that could turn upon themselves — shows the same marriage of practicality and richness that has marked the whole account of the doors: large enough to give worthy entrance to the Holy Place, yet contrived to move without unwieldiness, and adorned, when the following verses complete the picture, with the same carved cherubim and palms and flowers that ran over the walls and the inner doors, all overlaid with gold beaten to the shape of the carving. So the outer doorway, like the inner, preached by its very craftsmanship the character of the house. Even here, at the threshold between the porch and the Holy Place, nothing was left plain or common; the paradisal figures continued across the doors, the gold took the shape of every carved form, and the worshipping priest passed, morning and evening, through a gate wrought and gilded like the sanctuary it opened. Keil's forward reading gathers door, carving, and gilding into one movement, and prepares the reader for the last member of the structure the chapter will describe — the inner court that enclosed the whole.
35:
The carving of the outer doors with cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers, and their covering with gold fitted to the carved work, is recorded briefly, and Gill alone comments directly, noting that the figures were 'as upon the doors of the most holy place.' The synthesis of so short a verse must draw on that single cross-reference, the verse's own words, and the pattern the chapter has established. What Gill's brevity signals is continuity: the same paradisal imagery that adorned the olive-wood doors of the oracle — the winged guardians, the flourishing palms, the blossoms in full bloom — was repeated on the cypress doors of the Holy Place, so that both entrances bore the identical carved forms, and the whole house, from its inmost door to its outer, was of one decorative piece.

The gilding, too, followed the manner already described: the gold was 'fitted upon the carved work,' beaten thin and laid so closely that it took the shape of every raised cherub and incised palm, revealing the figures rather than burying them. As on the inner doors and the walls, the metal did not conceal the carving but conformed to it, the glory of the gold and the meaning of the sacred forms displayed together.

There is nothing here for the witnesses to debate, and honesty requires the synthesis to be plain that the verse repeats what has gone before. Yet the repetition itself carries weight. The book is at pains to show that the adornment of the sanctuary was uniform and thorough — the same figures, the same gilding, at every threshold and upon every wall — so that no part of the house fell short of the rest in richness or in meaning. The cherubim who overshadowed the ark within reappeared upon the doors that led toward it, and the palms and flowers that made the walls a garden ran across the entrances as well, until the worshipper met the imagery of paradise at every step of his approach. The doors of the Holy Place, opened daily by the ministering priests, thus carried the same message as the door of the oracle, opened yearly by the high priest alone: that this was the house of a holy God, a place made beautiful throughout with the figures of angelic attendance and of righteousness come to flower. Gill's short note, tying these doors to those of the most holy place, is enough to fix the point. The sanctuary was all of a piece, and its every gate preached, in carved cedar and beaten gold, the holiness and the beauty of the presence within.
36:
The inner court that enclosed the temple — three rows of hewn stone and a row of cedar beams — draws the witnesses into their last architectural dispute, over how the verse is to be understood, and into a rich set of historical notes on the courts of the house. Adam Clarke reads the phrase most literally, asking whether it means 'there were three courses of stone, and then one course of timber all through this wall' — 'three strata of stone and one stratum of timber, and so on' — and wondering aloud whether so alternating a construction 'could be very durable.' He connects the manner of building to the prophet's image of 'the stone crying out of the wall, and the beam out of the timber' answering it (Habakkuk 2:11), noting that here at least the timber, being in the inner court, was not exposed to the weather.

Ellicott weighs the same words and, with Josephus, prefers the other reading: not a floor raised on three courses of stone under a cedar planking, but a low enclosing wall 'of three courses of stone, with a coping of cedar wood.' His reason is liturgical and practical — in this court stood the altar of burnt offering and the laver, and the sacrifices 'could hardly have been done on a wooden pavement.' He then unfolds the whole system of courts: the inner or 'higher' court, the 'court of the priests,' doubled like all the temple's dimensions to about a hundred cubits by two hundred; an outer or 'great court' beyond it, referred to in the 'two courts' of 2 Kings 21:5 and the visions of Ezekiel 40:17; and, on Josephus's account, great cloisters and further substructures, the memory of which survived in the name 'Solomon's Porch.' Keil and Delitzsch agree that the inner court, the 'upper court' of Jeremiah 36:10, was set on higher ground and enclosed by a low wall of three stone courses and a row of cedar, whether laid horizontally or set upright as a palisade 'that the people might be able to see through into the court of the priests.'

The debate — floor or wall, and whether stone and timber alternated throughout — the witnesses leave genuinely open, and it is a debate about construction, not doctrine. Yet even here a truth surfaces. Ellicott and Keil both note that the low wall or open railing let the people, gathered in the outer court, see into the court of the priests, so that 'even under that dispensation,' as Matthew Henry observed of this same feature elsewhere in the chapter, the worshippers 'were not kept wholly either in the dark or at a distance.' The gradation of courts — outer for the people, inner and raised for the priests — expressed the ordered holiness of the old covenant, the nearness and the separation held together; and Ellicott's tracing of the tradition down to 'Solomon's Porch' quietly links the temple Solomon built to the courts where a greater than Solomon would one day walk and teach.
37:
The chapter turns to the chronology of the work, recording that the foundation of the house was laid 'in the fourth year, in the month Zif,' and the witnesses gather the notice together with its companion in the following verse to reckon the whole span of the building. Keil and Delitzsch read the two verses as one statement of the time consumed: the foundation laid in Solomon's fourth year in the month Ziv, the house finished in his eleventh year in the month Bul, 'so that it was built in seven years, or, more precisely, seven years and a half.' They pause, as is their manner, over the name of the month: Bul, they explain, 'signifies the fruit month, the month of tree fruits,' a name that 'probably originated with the Phoenicians,' with whom the fruit ripened later, and which is said to appear on the great Sidonian inscription.

Charles Ellicott adds the sense of the earlier month, noting that Zif, meaning 'the brightness of flowers,' 'corresponds to about May' — the season of blossom in which the great work began. Gill fixes the year plainly: 'the fourth year of Solomon's reign,' when the foundation was laid, matching the date given at the chapter's opening.

There is no dispute among the witnesses here; their concern is simply to establish the timetable exactly, and the synthesis, following them, dwells on what the timetable shows. The foundation was laid in the fourth year of the reign — the first three years, as Matthew Henry had observed of the opening verse, taken up in settling the affairs of the kingdom, so that the king 'might not find any embarrassment from them in this work.' The month was Zif, the bright season of flowers in the spring, a fitting time to begin a house whose walls would be carved with palms and open flowers, a sanctuary meant to be a paradise in cedar and gold. Keil's care over the meaning of the month-names is not idle antiquarianism; it shows the calendar of the ancient Near East, with its Phoenician 'fruit month' and its springtime 'brightness of flowers,' woven into the sacred record, and it quietly supports the point made at the chapter's start, that these borrowed month-names bespeak a book given its final shape in a later age. Above all, the verse marks the moment of beginning that the next verse will match with the moment of completion, framing the seven-and-a-half years of labour between two named months of two named years. The house that had been so long deferred — 480 years after the Exodus — was at last founded, in the spring of Solomon's fourth year, and the record fixes the date with the same precision it had shown at the first.
38:
The chapter closes as it opened, with a date, recording that in the eleventh year, in the month Bul, 'the house was finished throughout all the parts thereof, and according to all the fashion of it,' so that Solomon 'was seven years in building it.' The witnesses complete their reckoning of the time and reflect on the swiftness of so great a work. Keil and Delitzsch, gathering this verse with the last, confirm the span of 'seven years, or, more precisely, seven years and a half,' and observe that 'in comparison with other large buildings of antiquity, and also of modern times, the work was executed in a very short time' — noting, from Pliny, that the celebrated temple of Diana at Ephesus was two hundred and twenty years in the building.

Adam Clarke presses the same comparison and draws out the temple's true nature. He wonders that a house whose stones and timbers were all prepared beforehand should have taken seven years and six months, and answers that 'the ornamenting, gilding, making the carved work, cherubim, trees, flowers,' must have consumed the time. He fixes the months — Bul answering to part of October and November, as Zif to April and May — and notes that the dedication waited until the following year. Then he sets the temple beside the wonders of the heathen world: Diana's temple at Ephesus, 'one of the seven wonders,' and the pyramids of Egypt, built by three hundred and sixty thousand men over twenty years; yet, he insists, 'neither was the temple any such work as this.' The temple 'was never intended to hold a vast concourse of people; it was only for the service of the Lord,' the priests alone employed in it — 'it was never designed to be a place to worship in, but a place to worship at.' Gill marks the year as the eleventh of Solomon's reign, and Ellicott confirms the completion.

The witnesses do not divide, and their reflections converge on a single point that fitly ends the chapter. The work was great but swift, its speed owing, as Matthew Henry observed, to Solomon being 'in earnest in it,' having 'money enough' and 'nothing to divert him,' while 'many hands made quick work.' Yet the true measure of the temple was never its scale or its speed but its purpose. Clarke's distinction — a place to worship at rather than in — catches the character of the whole old dispensation: the house was a shrine for the priestly service and the appointed seat of the divine presence, before which the tribes came, not an assembly-hall for the multitude. Seven and a half years after its founding in the spring of the fourth year, and 480 years after the Exodus, the long-deferred house stood complete 'according to all the fashion of it,' every part finished as designed. What the dedication of the following chapter would celebrate, and what the whole structure was raised to foreshadow, is the dwelling of God with men — the shadow now finished in cedar and gold, awaiting the substance that a greater Son of David would one day bring.
